text.title: Customer Journey Mapping — 7-Step Process
text.summary: Structured procedure for creating research-grounded journey maps that expose cross-channel friction and drive prioritized improvements.

section.title: Step 1: Define Scope

rule.statement: Specify the persona, journey type, start point, end point, and channels in scope before beginning research.
rule.rationale: A journey map without a defined persona and scope conflates the experiences of different user types and collapses important variation. Scope drives which data to collect.

section.p: Questions: Which persona? Which journey (purchase, onboarding, support)? Defined start and end points? Which channels are in scope?
Journey types: Current state (diagnose problems), Future state (design target), Day-in-the-life (broader context), Service blueprint (includes backstage operations).

section.title: Step 2: Gather Research

rule.statement: Base the map on actual research data — interviews, observation, analytics, support tickets, surveys. Never map from assumption.
rule.rationale: A map built from internal assumptions reflects how the team thinks the product works, not how users experience it. Assumption-based maps reinforce existing blind spots rather than exposing them.

section.p: Data sources: user interviews, observational studies, analytics, support data, surveys, employee interviews from customer-facing roles.

section.title: Step 3: Identify Stages

rule.statement: Define 4-8 major stages that represent distinct phases of the user's experience. Each stage should have a clear user goal.
rule.rationale: Fewer than 4 stages over-compresses the experience and hides friction. More than 8 stages produce unmaintainable maps that teams cannot use in workshops.

section.p: Example e-commerce journey: Awareness → Research → Purchase → Delivery → Use → Support.

section.title: Step 4: Map Actions and Touchpoints

rule.statement: Document what users do (actions) and where they interact with the product/service (touchpoints) separately for each stage.
rule.rationale: Conflating actions and touchpoints obscures whether friction is in user behavior or in a specific channel. Separate rows allow targeted intervention.

section.reference: Full map table with rows for actions, touchpoints, thoughts, emotions, pain points

rule.statement: Record emotional state and cognitive questions at each stage — not what the user should feel, but what research shows they actually feel.
rule.rationale: Emotion curves reveal where user confidence drops or anxiety spikes — these are the highest-leverage intervention points. Assuming positive emotion at product touchpoints is the most common mapping error.

section.title: Step 6: Identify Pain Points and Opportunities

rule.statement: Distinguish pain points (current friction) from opportunities (actionable improvements). Every pain point should have at least one opportunity attached.
rule.rationale: Pain points without opportunities produce a list of problems with no forward motion. Opportunities without pain points are speculation. The pairing anchors design work to diagnosed need.

section.title: Step 7: Visualize and Prioritize

rule.statement: Prioritize opportunities by combination of user impact (frequency and severity of pain) and organizational effort to address.
rule.rationale: Unprioritized opportunity lists produce no action — teams debate importance without a framework. Impact-effort prioritization produces a decision, not a discussion.

section.reference: Per-stage detail template for deep-dive documentation
